utterances: Yeah, let's jump in.
It's super cramped.
Rita Kietzer looks dubious.
But not to worry - for an extra $200 dollars on top of our thousand-dollar ticket, the smuggler will sell us a life vest.
He holds up an actual example of what smugglers are selling in the Mediterranean.
Except...
It's fake life jacket.
It's not real.
You - if you had that on, it would actually pull you under the water?
Oh, my God.
Next station - we're at a refugee camp now, checking out the facilities - the latrine we're going to share with 50 other people...
It's basically is a hole with a plastic plate over it.
This can filled with the very little amount of water we're going to be issued every day...
This is for showering, for bathing, tooth-brushing, cooking, everything - only two gallons.
The small tent we're going to live in with two, maybe three, other families.
Ooh (ph).
Hot, huh?
This is really how it feels from inside.
And every time we moved to a new station, we're asked to give up one more of our possession cards.
This lady and I both gave up our medicine at the same time.
All I have left is my passport (laughter).
What goes through your head as you decide what to keep?
It's very scary, losing a little part of yourself each time.
As the tour concludes, Abdalrazag says he has one final thought to share.
No one should live this life.
Please, he says, it's not ok.
Thank you.
(APPLAUSE)
Thank you for coming.
Nurith Aizenman, NPR News.

Before Hurricane Stan struck the Central American coast last Tuesday, forecasters were calling for five to 15 inches of rain; almost 30 inches fell in some areas of Guatemala, highlighting the difficulty of hurricane-related forecasts.
We're gonna talk about the art, the science and the politics of predicting hurricanes and what it will take to do a better job.
If you have ideas or questions, give us a call.
Our number is (800) 989-8255.
That's (800) 989-TALK.
And the e-mail address is totn@npr.org.
And joining us now is Debbie Cenziper, a reporter for The Miami Herald.
Her paper conducted an eight-month investigation into the National Hurricane Center and whether US agencies could better predict the location and intensities of hurricanes and landfall.
She joins us from the studios at The Miami Herald.
And it's good to have you on TALK OF THE NATION.
Good to be here.
It seems in recent years the track of hurricane storm predictions has actually been pretty good.
What's the problem?

Well, I think what's happening is that at critical times there's basic equipment that's not available to forecasters, things like buoys and weather balloons and specially equipped hurricane hunter planes, and that's affected them, you know, as storms were making landfall.
Can you give me a for instance?
Sure.

During Hurricane Katrina that--as the storm was strengthening in the Atlantic, the Hurricane Center opted, because of budget problems, to only fly their Gulfstream jet one time before the storm hit south Florida, and on that flight a whole bunch of--there was a computer crash, so valuable information never made it to forecasters or their computer models, and some people believe that potentially affected the forecasts in south Florida.
And we, of course, remember what Katrina did in Louisiana and Mississippi, but south Florida suffered some severe damage as well.
That's right.
In fact, at landfall, the storm dipped deep into Miami-Dade County.
A lot of people weren't expecting that.
Another factor that your series highlights is difficulties in figuring the intensity of storms, and the example you use there was Hurricane Charley, which hit Florida last year.
That's right.
It was the strongest storm to hit Florida since Andrew in '92, and what we found is that as Charley was approaching Florida, three coastal weather observing stations along the coast weren't working.
They were malfunctioning.
Special--two planes that the Hurricane Center uses to measure wind speeds at the surface of a storm were unavailable because the government had sent them on unrelated research missions.
Weather balloons were not launched, you know, in the Caribbean as the storm was approaching Florida.
So, yeah, there were a number of things that were down at that time.
When you talk about those, those are the famous hurricane hunter planes.
That's right.

They're planes flown by the US Air--the Reserves, and then NOAA, the federal government, has three planes, one jet that flies in the environment around the storm, and two turboprop planes, all specially equipped, all with equipment that's not on the--you know, the Air Force planes, and often those planes are not available either because they're diverted for unrelated research missions or because the Hurricane Center is worried about breaking the bank.
They only get, you know, a certain number of flight hours every year.

And obviously this could lead not only to errors of--like Charley, which hit in a different place and was rather stronger than some anticipated, though the Weather Service will say it was within the cone of where it was predicted to be--but it could also lead to the crying wolf storms where everybody says evacuate and then it turns out not to be so big.
I was wondering if you could address Jim's question.
You know, what difference does it make?
The warnings seem to be timely and accurate.
The Trump administration gave a little ground today in its dispute with Chinese tech giant Huawei.
It is backing off on restrictions placed on the company less than a week ago.
The Commerce Department had barred U.S.
companies from doing business with Huawei unless they received a special license.
The idea was to hurt Huawei, but U.S.
tech company stocks were caught in the crossfire.
NPR's Jackie Northam has been following developments.
She's back in the studio.
Hello again, Jackie.
Hi, Mary Louise.
All right.
So why is the Commerce Department backing off these restrictions so quickly, just put in place in the last week?
Right.
Well, they are backing off.
It's a 90-day reprieve.
So in other words, Huawei can continue to buy components from U.S.
companies, whether it be computer chips or software, anything like that.
Analysts told me that it was the U.S.

tech companies that were going to take a beating here because they have so much business with Huawei and that they've been passing on that message to President Trump that they were going to get hit hard.
Stocks in two major computer chip companies - Qualcomm and Broadcom, two major ones - were down 6% today.
Google dropped 2%.
And this was after Google said it would stop supporting Android devices for phones made by Huawei because the company was on the Commerce Department's blacklist.
Google is a major software provider for Huawei.
And if these restrictions went on for months, Google could take a beating.
Correct me if I'm wrong here, Jackie, but didn't the U.S.
back off last year in a somewhat similar way shortly after blacklisting another Chinese telecom company?
That's right.
It was a company called ZTE, and they were on the blacklist.
And it went on for a while.
And they were on the verge of bankruptcy.
And this is a major Chinese corporation - almost went bust until the Trump administration reversed that decision and allowed U.S.
companies to supply it again.
So it's the same sort of scenario.
But, Mary Louise, the Trump administration has been tightening the screws on Huawei for months now in part because it has links to the Chinese government.
And U.S.
intelligence agencies believe Huawei could use its equipment to spy on the U.S.
and its allies.
The administration asked Canada to arrest and extradite one of Huawei's senior officials.
And administration officials have been fanning out across the world asking allies, pressuring allies not to use Huawei equipment.
So up until now, it's been a full-court press by the administration to contain the company.
Yeah.
It sounds like a fascinating back-and-forth, how to hurt Chinese companies without harming U.S.
companies in the balance.
I mean, you could have described this until a few hours ago, as it sounded like almost all-out war between the U.S.
and Huawei.
Are we now at a 90-day truce?
Maybe a cease-fire.

The decision to blacklist Huawei was seen by many - in the beginning, anyway - as a bargaining ploy in the overall trade war between China and the U.S., where there are increasing tariffs on each other's products.
And it was seen as an effort to gain leverage over Beijing.
One analyst I talked to just in the past couple hours says easing the restrictions on Huawei now may be an opportunity for both sides to save face and get back to the negotiating table.
But we'll see if that works.
